speaker: interviewee
text: OK, July the 11th, 1995. The survivor is Joseph Kempler. Interviewer Larry Herbst, Glendale, California, USA. It will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Larry Herbst. I'm here to interview Joseph Kempler. It's July 11th, 1995. We're in Los Angeles, California-- Glendale to be specific.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Joseph Kempler. I was born on April 12, 1928, which makes me 67 years old. And I was born in Krakow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mr. Kempler, tell me, if you would, what it was like to be a young boy in Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a part of a Jewish family. We lived in the Kazimierz, which was the Jewish center of the town, an old 14th-century type part of town. It was a family, my father and my mother, a sister and a brother. I was going to school. It was a very good private school. It was a school that the morning sessions were Hebrew subjects and religious subjects. In the afternoon it was the general subjects. It was an all-day, very intensive school. I was an excellent student, if I may say so myself. And I did everything, what normal boys would do, I mean, whether it's sports, activities, and friends, and doing things. One thing that I loved above everything else was books. So I spent a lot of time reading. My world really was that of books. The reality of the world was books. Whatever was in a book was something which I believed. It has a great influence with the rest of my life. This is the reason I mentioned it now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was quite a normal, pleasant life. What were your favorite books?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were-- I would read anything, anything from adventures to science. One series-- one author, which was my particular favorite-- I don't know the-- is Karl May, and Karl May was incidentally Hitler's favorite as well. He was writing adventure stories, primarily from the West, as well as the other parts of the world. So he was my favorite. But anybody from Jules Verne or science books, whatever I put my fingers on I would read. I really had, for all practical purposes, a photographic memory. I could look at a-- scan a page in a book and remember it for years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was reading a lot. And my learning was primarily that of books. Was your house filled with books?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. As a matter of fact, most of the books came from library. There were private libraries. So, no. My family, except for my sister, were not readers. Just she and I were the readers. As a matter of fact, she introduced me to the idea of reading books, starting maybe at the age of five.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I would say it was a normal life by the standards that I knew at that time. I mean, living in Poland was not that easy. There was still a great deal of anti-Semitism-- [? there ?] still, but I mean this was the normal reaction to much of Polish population. The life was not easy. But that's all I knew, and I was perfectly happy, until the war started when I was 11. OK. Tell me a little bit about your father, what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Max Kempler. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't really know so much about my family, as I lost them too early. But I understand that he came from a small town in Poland. And it was the practice in those days, at the age of 13 after bar mitzvah, young men would go off and make a life for themselves outside. And he traveled to Germany, as far as I know, where he was working in the restaurant business, waitering, and all kinds of similar activities. He came back. He was in partners on hotel and a coffee house business, which went bankrupt. And then he finally, when I came around, he had a small restaurant in Krakow. So this was the source of his livelihood. A quiet man. I never was very close to him, particularly. He was very strict. But he was quite religious. And he led me in this direction, as far as the religion and the synagogue and so on-- even though, after my attendance in school after a few years, I knew far more than he did. So I became sort of leading him. At that time, of course, I was at the age, already, when I knew it all. So, well, he was hard working, very hard working person. Totally committed, totally devoted. I mean work went above everything. And he was also-- he wanted his family-- he used to use a phrase, 'Zusammen leben'-- keeping together, living together. So this was very important to him, to keep the family together. But he was absent more than he was present because of his work, six or seven days a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did ever spend any time in the restaurant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I came there very often, sometimes helping out one way or the other. So I was-- I was-- yeah, I spent quite a bit of time there after school, maybe a bringing him lunches. It was not too far from where we lived. So yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the clientele like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It varied from people who were loyal customers-- usually Jewish-- to people who were-- came in off the streets, and who wanted to have a drink-- a drink or something quick to eat. In the backroom, also, he had billiard tables. So people used to come and play billiards. He used to have a wind-up phonograph that I was in charge of. And we used to play music. So it was this kind of an atmosphere. I sort of liked it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the restaurant?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think it had a name. At least I don't remember. As far as I know, it was just a storefront. And there was no name, except I believe it did have the name of my father over the top, sort of not clear.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your mother? What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- her name was Marka. People called her Manya. Very quiet, fine person, really-- I was very close to her. She was the type that always calm, always collected. She was the-- the doer, the peacemaker. She ran the house really, and did it very well. Took care of the three of us. I understand from my sister, who is-- I'll tell you later, she's still alive and much older-- that she lost two children-- one before I was born, and one after I was born, which has affected her very, very seriously during the time, you know, emotionally distraught by the loss of the children, and fear for me. She became sort of overprotective. In some respects I think it has caused me some form of a trauma, which I can't put my finger on it. But I was extremely close to her. I was always concerned someway about losing her, even as a small child. So I was sort of, from the very beginning, the idea of loss of mother long before the war came, it was sort of a factor in my life. I'm not clear why. So we got along fine, very, very close. I mean, maybe closer than is average.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your family home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] second story. It had-- you entered through the hallway. And to the right was the kitchen. Moving forward in the hallway, there were two bedrooms, left and right; a bathroom; and a bath which never worked. As a matter of fact, we were there in 1989, visited, and we got into the apartment. I mean, that exactly. There was a Polish woman living there. And I said, I remember the bathrooms didn't work. She says, it still doesn't work. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So this place has not changed. It has the same tiles and the paint, the same smell inside the house, the-- I don't mean in the apartment, but in the house itself. It was unbelievable. And my nose led me right there without-- without being lost. I knew exactly where it was. I have many memories associated with it. Looking back on it, it was dismal. At that time was only home I knew. It was fine. And there were three of you children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister's name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judith, Judy, or Junka in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the age difference between you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was 17 years older, is 17 years older than I am. She was my half sister-- is my half sister. My father's first wife died. She has-- so he had two children, my brother and my sister. And then he married my mother and I was the third one. My sister was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as far as I know-- she was-- she was working, and she was always playing and going skiing and having boyfriends. I mean, this kind of a life. And she fell in love with a musician, a German Jew, who was traveling all over the world. As a matter of fact, I don't know if the name Rosner means anything to you. This was one of the key men in He was a musician in the with the famous violin. He was playing in his orchestra. So she fell in love with him. And took off. And married in 1936. Took off to Lebanon, and traveled the world, and had a good time. My brother, on the other hand, as was the tradition in-- in those days, young men had to learn the profession. And so the father decided-- so they would go to a [INAUDIBLE] to apprentice. And this was the way they learned the profession. He, as far as I know, he was an artist. Whatever he did, I mean, whether it was writing, or [INAUDIBLE] handwriting, or whatever he did, was a masterpiece. There was something about him. He hated to be a tailor. It was not in him. But he had no choice. And he became a master tailor, which had a-- also an effect later on. And whatever he sewed was something which was a masterpiece, truly. So he became quite well known. And then during the war it's helped him for a while anyway. But he was a frustrated boy, because he didn't want to do what his father thought-- our father thought he should be doing. But he resigned himself to that. And I think he got pleasure in knowing that what he was doing was really something that he could be creative. I got along well with my sister. We were very close as well down to this day. She is-- she always was. She took me as sort of being her protege in many respects. And occasionally we'd visit her when she was traveling various places in Poland and so on. And so we were very close. So she was the second in the family after my mother. So the women in my family was like the ones I was close to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rather than the men.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rather than the men, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adolf Dolleck, as he was called. And he was 11 years older than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friends were school friends. I was the-- the school I went to was an all boys school. So I never knew any girls. I had one girlfriend whom I met [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through the family of my sister's husband. So I met her when I was nine, and she was seven. And then I used to see her once in a while. I guess I was in love with her, because I-- whenever I went to see her, I would wash my knees. And everybody teased me. And I really didn't know why, but this makes sense today. And she also-- well this-- I'll never be able to get to this-- but she also figured during the war, sort of significantly in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anita Lamper.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a scene that he was using young girls to polish shelves because of their long hands, long fingers, and so on, so they could get inside ammunition shelves that he was making.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the original book, there is a story of how she was challenged-- or he was challenged, why he would use children. She was 13 years old at the time. And he said it was because of the fingers and all that. And she was called out to the Appellplatz in Plaszow to demonstrate. So it's a-- just an aside. But she was the one who was saved to Schindler. She was in Brinnlitz in What about the other children in the neighborhood? What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The children-- the neighborhood had the-- a Jewish population, but it's also mixed with Poles. And they were sort of mostly very anti-Semitic. I had no friends at all, except the school friends. And so there were very few in the immediate neighborhood. There were some streets away. So I had quite a few friends, but all of them were from school.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say they were anti-Semitic, what kinds of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for instance, they would-- anything from throwing rocks to just singing all kinds of jeering type song, you know, about about Moses and Abraham, and all kinds of little things that were insulting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the songs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were saying-- let me see if I remember. [NON-ENGLISH], which means, don't touch my beard, and stuff like this. Various little type of little ditties, which were unpleasant. But even this was sort of taken for granted. This was part of life. So as a child these things were not questioned. This is the way it was. There was nothing to compare it with, so this was the way we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it-- it didn't particularly disturb me, but by the same token, I didn't seek any associations with them. Besides, it was generally frowned upon, you know, the Jewish people would associate with other Jewish people. And usually friends were carefully selected. Now, your family was religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in a sense of having a kosher home and going to the synagogues on Friday night and on Saturday and all the holidays, and observing all of the traditions. It wasn't Orthodox in a Polish sense-- A Polish Jewish sense. It was not of a case of observing all the traditions which were expected, whether or not, in any way, Hassidic. Or I mean, my father didn't grow a beard or anything like this. But it was a traditional Jewish home. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the temple you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was one of the-- the old synagogues. As a matter of fact, it was called the old synagogue, is where we went on the high holidays, which is Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur; these kinds of holidays where you had to have a paid seat in order to go in there. But normal Friday night activities-- also Saturdays, usually, but not always, Friday night there was a local synagogue right around the corner. So this is where we went. I don't remember the names of any of them. The old-- the old synagogue, I believe, is being rebuilt in Krakow. The other one is gone, the small one.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was it? How many people would it hold? Or how many would come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one, the big one?

speaker: interviewer
text: The local.

speaker: interviewee
text: The local one, I wouldn't remember. But I would put it maybe 60 people-- 50, 60. Something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware at all of the goings on in Germany at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as a matter of fact, this was part of my reading. There was a book published about what was happening in Germany. It was pictures. It was published about 1938, and so it terrified me. So I knew what was going on. Then, since my sister's husband was of a German background, his family lived in Germany, very well-to-do Jews. And in 1939, Hitler took most of the Jewish-- German Jews and left them on the Polish border. And so they migrated down gradually. And they came down to Krakow, and they lived in synagogues and schools, and so on. There were place for them to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even before the war things became quite well known as to what was going on. And I was informed, maybe better than most people because of my reading again. It was a kind of a book that my mother didn't want me to read. But I read it anyway. Did you talk to your family about-- about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably. I don't remember, though. Probably so. Even though in most cases I did not much share as to what I was reading. It was all for me. Most people were not interested. They had-- their activities were different than mine. And I was absorbed in the book. But this was certainly-- maybe not before '37 or '38. '38 maybe. I was 10 years old, when I began to know what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had the war not happened, did you have some idea of the direction your life was taking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I always thought-- I mean people, normally when you have a small kid in a family ask them what they're going to be when they grow up. It's a standard question. I wanted to be an engineer. But I didn't know what kind. To me, engineering meant sort of some form of building, or architects for some reason. And the reason for that was, I think, that the only toys I had was a few wooden blocks that I would forever play with. So this sort of, people think, well, since you are playing with the blocks, you're probably going to be in construction. But nothing-- this wasn't reality. This was too early to really put it in any direction. If I stayed, the war didn't happen, I would have graduated from the school. Universities and so on were very difficult for Jews to attend. However, this probably would have been my direction. And I think that my parents, rather than trying to get me to learn some form of a practical profession, I think they were impressed with my learning abilities. And I think they had some plans along these lines. I know the school we went to was rather expensive. And I remember there were discussions about taking me out because of money. However, they always managed to [? do ?] that because they were interested in my advancement.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall of the first day of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember this exactly. The night before, there was inkling that war was going to come. And people would buy sticky tape, about one inch wide, which you put on the windows in the X patterns. The idea was that if the bomb would hit, the windows would not shatter. So the whole town was gluing these windows. The-- for some weeks before, they were digging air raid shelters-- zig-zag types, in the parks and so on, which I participated in. So there was a war fever. But Polish people had sort of a bravado attitude. Oh, we're going to beat Hitler. Don't worry about it; this kind of thing. But the soldiers were marching. My brother, incidentally, was drafted. And he was in the Polish army at that time. So the-- the war [INAUDIBLE] were happening for at least two months or so before that. On that Friday morning, September 1, I woke up with the sound of many planes. I was thinking, oh, they must be some from maneuvers or exercises. And then we heard the sounds of the bombs falling-- not too many, but there were quite a bit of noise. I was all excited about it. In those days, I had no fear. Everything was an adventure. So after that, I went to a friend of mine, who lived further down towards the center of town. And I was visiting with him. And during that time, that a German Stuka plane was flew very low, about to bomb. And then the tower, there's an old building, had a tower. There was a machine gun in place. You could see right there how they were firing at the plane. And I had a wonderful time with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that point, the war, as real as it was, did not have these dark meanings or dark clouds on the horizon yet. Too young and stupid to know what was going on. So I remember that day. Then it got worse with the bombing, and people began to run, run away. And Germans were approaching. And we spent a lot of time in the basement, in a shelter.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't want to go anyplace. The people who ran, mostly ran east. And some escapes-- some escaped into Russia at that time. But we stayed until the Germans came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when they arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember this was Wednesday the 6th. These-- these things stick pretty closely in my mind. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They marched in. And my father and I, he took me for a walk, just to see what was going on. And the Germans were marching and setting up the narrow streets in Krakow. They were setting up one-way signs and all kinds of other things which didn't exist before and directed traffic. I mean, this was very exciting. At this point, there was no problem at all, as far as the-- the German army interfering.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we walked down there, and looked at some of the bombed houses. He wanted to get some form of a license-- beer license, or something like this at that day. So it was sort of, well, now Germans are in. And we go about our life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember my-- my tragedy that day was that I had a watch-- my first watch. And I had pulled the stem out. It stuck in my mind as the-- the bad part of the day. It's interesting, because things happened so gradually that you start from the-- this kind of a basis. It's just unimaginable how things have really changed, and how fast they began to change. The next day, I saw down the street the German soldiers took some Jewish people with beards, and were cutting them off with scissors, cutting them off sometimes on one side only, or the other. I remember there was one Jewish man, Hassidic. And they cut his beard, and at the same time cut into his face and was bleeding. It sort of shocked me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the beginning was not necessarily bad. This kind of activity, seeing something got beat up, things of this type-- as far as I was concerned. But I know many people who were arrested-- some of the intelligentsia and other people were being arrested right away. But somehow this was something which did not involve me personally.

speaker: interviewee
text: I still went to school. The school was open until December, or end of November. So I still went about it. I just began my sixth grade. And things were going sort of normal. And then came the changes. Began-- began to happen very rapidly afterwards. December 4th, I remember, was one of those raid-- the Germans called it the Razzia-- where they surrounded the whole area and would go from house to house and take away possessions, whether it was furs, or jewelry, or money, or things of this type.
